bootstrap.author: SixStringBudget
bootstrap.title: Sold a guitar this weekend. First time ever. I can still feel its ghost on the wall
bootstrap.body:
Seven 'last guitars' in, I finally did the uncomfortable math: my playing improved more from one month of actual lessons than from the last three purchases combined. So the one I never reached for went to a kid who's going to play it every day, and the money is going to my teacher instead of the classifieds.

I'd like to report enlightenment but mostly I keep looking at the empty hook. Growth is stupid and hard.

comments.author: reversecowbell
comments.body: In the band days our lead player bought a two thousand dollar amp for a band that averaged eleven people a show. The tone was genuinely incredible. All eleven people agreed.

replies.author: SixStringBudget
replies.body: eleven people with impeccable taste though

comments.author: vinyl_endgame
comments.body: I'll defend the object a little — an instrument with history in your hands is not nothing. But the object only matters if you touch it. A guitar on a hook is furniture with strings.

comments.author: b_side_believer
comments.body: hot take, or maybe just a take: gear forums turned a generation of players into collectors. nobody's tone was ever fixed by a ninth pedal, but the forums can't sell you practice.

replies.author: reversecowbell
replies.body: guilty as charged. I can name more pickups than I can name chords.

bootstrap.author: vinyl_endgame
bootstrap.title: Streaming gave me every album ever recorded and somehow I listen to less
bootstrap.body:
Every record ever made, in my pocket, and last month I really listened to maybe five of them. Infinite choice turns out to be a kind of silence.

A record asks you to choose once and then commit for a whole side. No skipping without standing up, no queue to fiddle with. The crackle is a signature on a contract. I'm not saying everyone needs a turntable — I'm saying the friction was doing something, and we sanded it all off without asking what.

comments.author: b_side_believer
comments.body: the deeper problem is that the algorithm is a singles machine. it learns the hit and feeds you forty versions of the hit. track seven never stood a chance in that economy, and track seven is where the actual album lives.

replies.author: vinyl_endgame
replies.body: track seven is exactly where they keep the good heartbreak. this is known.

comments.author: SixStringBudget
comments.body: Vinyl fixed my listening and wrecked my wallet. New pressings run forty bucks around here — my whole streaming sub costs one record a month. And yet the forty dollar one gets listened to properly and the infinite library gets scrolled. Can't explain it, can confirm it.

comments.author: reversecowbell
comments.body: My old band's EP exists on 300 vinyl copies, about 270 of which are in my mom's basement. Our streaming numbers are also roughly 300. I can report with authority that the format was never our problem.

bootstrap.author: reversecowbell
bootstrap.title: What's a song you love that you absolutely cannot defend?
bootstrap.body:
Mine is Steal My Sunshine by Len. Canadian one-hit wonder, sounds the way a gas station slushie tastes, and when it comes on I am nineteen again and completely unstoppable.

No judgement zone. Empty your pockets.

comments.author: vinyl_endgame
comments.body: I reject the premise that pleasure needs a defence — but also mine is a certain Eurodance single from 1993 that I keep filed behind the jazz section so guests don't see it. So.

comments.author: SixStringBudget
comments.body: MMMBop. I'm a guitarist, I'm contractually supposed to say something with a nine minute solo. It's MMMBop.

replies.author: reversecowbell
replies.body: counterpoint: MMMBop is a genuinely great pop song and you've accidentally posted a defensible pick. try again.

comments.author: b_side_believer
comments.body: the whole concept of the guilty pleasure implies critics get a vote in your joy. they don't. the guilt is the only indefensible part.

comments.author: half_marathon_harold
comments.body: whatever cursed eurodance track is attached to my 5k personal best. I don't like it, I fear it. it comes on around kilometre four and my legs sign a contract without consulting me.

bootstrap.author: b_side_believer
bootstrap.title: Name an album where a non-single buries every single. I'll start: Rumours
bootstrap.body:
The Chain was never released as a single, and it's the best thing on Rumours by a comfortable margin. The label picked what the radio would swallow, not what was true.

This happens constantly. The single is the handshake, the deep cut is the conversation. Make your case for one.

comments.author: reversecowbell
comments.body: Zeppelin famously wouldn't even release singles in the UK — they wanted the album taken whole. So technically the entire catalogue is deep cuts. Checkmate, radio.

comments.author: SixStringBudget
comments.body: Achilles Last Stand. Ten and a half minutes, no radio station was ever going to touch it, and it's some of the best playing Page ever committed to tape. Deep cuts are where guitarists show off for other guitarists.

replies.author: b_side_believer
replies.body: 'deep cuts are where guitarists show off for other guitarists' — this is the thesis of my whole personality, thank you

comments.author: vinyl_endgame
comments.body: A producer once told me the second slot on side two is where they bury the songs they love too much to spend as singles. Almost certainly a myth. I believe it completely and I check every record I buy.

drip.author: reversecowbell
drip.title: A song I don't even LIKE has been stuck in my head for three days. Science, explain
drip.body:
It's a chorus I would skip on purpose. Three days. In the band we called these barnacle songs — they attach in transit and you cannot scrape them off.

Someone give me the exorcism protocol.

comments.author: vinyl_endgame
comments.body: the folk cure actually works for me: play the whole song once, start to finish, with attention. earworms are unfinished business. the loop is your brain trying to complete a side it only got a fragment of.

replies.author: reversecowbell
replies.body: so what you're saying is I have to look it in the eye. fine. FINE.

comments.author: b_side_believer
comments.body: notice it's never the verse. the chorus is engineered to hook and the verse is where the song actually is. you're not haunted by the song, you're haunted by its marketing department.

comments.author: SixStringBudget
comments.body: occupational hazard version: mine get stuck in my hands. I'll be noodling a riff for twenty minutes before my wife names the song and ruins my evening.

drip.author: SixStringBudget
drip.title: PSA for every adult who 'always wanted to learn an instrument': the window didn't close
drip.body:
I started taking guitar seriously at 31 after a decade of owning guitars, which is a different hobby. Three things I wish someone had said sooner:

Fifteen minutes a day beats a heroic Sunday session. Learn songs you love, not the ones the method book insists build character. And being bad at something on purpose is genuinely great for you — it's the only part of my week with no stakes at all.

The kid-prodigy thing is a myth that mostly sells lessons to parents. Adults learn fine. We just quit louder.

comments.author: kettlebellkate
comments.body: This is word for word the speech I give at the gym. Consistency beats intensity, and 'too late' is a story people tell to avoid being a beginner in public. Started lifting from absolute zero at 38. Only regret is the years I spent deciding.

replies.author: SixStringBudget
replies.body: 'being a beginner in public' is exactly the fear, and it evaporates about two weeks in when you realize nobody is watching

comments.author: b_side_believer
comments.body: seconding 'learn songs you love'. the method book playlist is how they get you to quit. three chords and one song you'd defend in an argument will carry you through the first year.

comments.author: vinyl_endgame
comments.body: My dad took up piano at 60. He is, and I say this with love, not good. It's also the best hour of his week and the house sounds alive when I visit. Highly recommend everyone be worse at more things.

drip.author: b_side_believer
drip.title: Unpopular here, apparently nowhere else I say it: live albums are better than studio albums
drip.body:
The studio version is the thesis. The live version is the defence, and songs tell the truth on stage. Tempos get honest, arrangements loosen their collar, and the crowd becomes an instrument the band has to answer to.

A band that can't outplay their own record is a recording project, and that's fine, but it's a different thing.

comments.author: reversecowbell
comments.body: From the stage side: completely true, and sometimes the truth hurts. Our songs all gained about 20 bpm live because of a little-known production technique called fear.

replies.author: b_side_believer
replies.body: 'fear' is the greatest producer in live music history and has never once been credited

comments.author: vinyl_endgame
comments.body: A good live pressing gives you two textures at once — the crackle of the record and the roar of the room. It's a recording of a night that actually happened, imperfections included. Of course I'm in.

comments.author: SixStringBudget
comments.body: Half agree. But some albums are studio constructions that were never meant to survive contact with a stage — you can't gig Pet Sounds with four guys and a van. The studio IS the instrument on records like that.

drip.author: vinyl_endgame
drip.title: Tonight's assignment: one album, front to back, phone in the other room. Who's in?
drip.body:
Pick something you claim to love and give it the forty-five minutes it asked for. No shuffle, no skips, no second screen. Side break is for stretching and turning the record over, or for staring meaningfully out a window if you're on digital.

Report back with what you heard that you'd never heard before. There will be something. There always is.

comments.author: b_side_believer
comments.body: this is the only correct way to meet an album. sequencing is an argument the artist is making, and shuffle interrupts them mid-sentence.

comments.author: SixStringBudget
comments.body: Did this a while back with a record I've 'known' for twenty years and heard a countermelody in the second verse that I'd somehow never once noticed. Twenty years. It was just sitting there being beautiful without me.

replies.author: vinyl_endgame
replies.body: and that's the sermon, folks. they're all just sitting there being beautiful without us.

comments.author: reversecowbell
comments.body: in. and pouring one out for track sequencing, a dying art. we spent longer arguing about our EP's running order than we spent recording it, and we were right to.

late_comments.author: SixStringBudget
late_comments.body: Update: lesson money officially deployed. My teacher asked what I want to work on and I said 'everything' and he sighed like a man at the end of a very long week. We're starting with rhythm. Apparently I needed to start with rhythm.

late_comments.author: reversecowbell
late_comments.body: This thread has quietly become my gym playlist and I want everyone to know that Steal My Sunshine into somebody's shameful Eurodance pick is a genuinely elite transition. No regrets, moderate shame.

late_comments.author: vinyl_endgame
late_comments.body: Checking back in: two friends told me this week they played an album front to back for the first time in years. The friction is returning. The movement grows.

late_comments.author: b_side_believer
late_comments.body: day five and not one person has shown up to defend a single over its album's best deep cut. the motion carries. track seven wins by acclamation.
